content.problemStatement:
There is a matrix of size N×MN \times MN×M. Two **distinct** cells of the matrix (x1,y1)(x_1, y_1)(x1​,y1​) and (x2,y2)(x_2, y_2)(x2​,y2​) are painted with colors 1\text{1}1 and 2\text{2}2 respectively.

You have to paint the **remaining** cells of the matrix such that:


No two adjacent cells are painted in same color.
Each color is an integer from 111 to 10910^9109 (both inclusive).
The number of **distinct** colors used is **minimum** (including 1\text{1}1 and 2\text{2}2).
**Note**:


You cannot repaint the cells (x1,y1)(x_1, y_1)(x1​,y1​) and (x2,y2)(x_2, y_2)(x2​,y2​).
Two cells are adjacent if they share a common edge. Thus, for a cell (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) there are 444 adjacent cells. The adjacent cells are (x,y+1),(x,y−1),(x+1,y),(x, y+1), (x, y-1), (x+1,y),(x,y+1),(x,y−1),(x+1,y), and (x−1,y)(x-1,y)(x−1,y).

Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow. Each test case consists of 333 lines.
The first line contains two space seperated integers NNN and MMM denoting the dimensions of the matrix.
The second line contains two integers x1x_1x1​ and y1y_1y1​, denoting the coordinates of cell which is painted 1\text{1}1.
The third line contains two integers x2x_2x2​ and y2y_2y2​, denoting the coordinates of cell which is painted 2\text{2}2.


Output Format
For each test case, output NNN lines, each consisting of MMM space separated integers where jthj^{th}jth integer in ithi^{th}ith line denotes the color of cell in ithi^{th}ith row and jthj^{th}jth column of the matrix and must be in [1,109][1, 10^9][1,109] inclusive.


In case of multiple possible answers, output any.
constraints: Sum of N·M over all test cases ≤ 10^5
All colors must be integers in [1, 10^9]
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with two integers N and M – the number of rows and columns of the matrix.
- A line with two integers x1 y1 – the coordinates (1‑based) of the cell already painted with color 1.
- A line with two integers x2 y2 – the coordinates (1‑based) of the cell already painted with color 2.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines. Each line contains M space‑separated integers – the colors of the cells of that row. The output must respect the constraints described in the statement.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111:

The initially painted cells are (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and (2,2)(2,2)(2,2). One possible way is to paint the remaining cells with color 444. This way, no two adjacent cells have the same color.
Note that, instead of color 444, we can use any other color in the range [1,109][1, 10^9][1,109] except colors 111 and 222.
The number of distinct colors used in this case is 333. It can be shown that the minimum number of distinct colors cannot be less than 333.
Test Case 222:

The initially painted cells are (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and (1,2)(1,2)(1,2). One possible way is to paint the remaining cells as shown in the figure. This way, no two adjacent cells have the same color.
The number of distinct colors used in this case is 222. It can be shown that the minimum number of distinct colors cannot be less than 222.

solution.approach:
The grid is a bipartite graph: cells with (row+col) even form one part, cells with (row+col) odd form the other. Adjacent cells always belong to different parts. Therefore a proper coloring needs at most two colors, one for each part. The two pre‑colored cells may lie on the same part or on different parts.

* If they are on different parts (parities differ) we can keep the given colors for their parts and use exactly two distinct colors for the whole matrix.
* If they are on the same part, they already use two different colors on that part. All cells of the opposite part must avoid both of these colors, so we need a third color for the whole opposite part. The remaining cells of the first part can reuse either of the two existing colors because they are never adjacent to each other.

Thus the minimum number of distinct colors is 2 when the given cells have opposite parity, otherwise it is 3. Construction is straightforward and runs in O(N·M) per test case.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N, M, (x1, y1), (x2, y2).
2. Compute p1 = (x1 + y1) % 2, p2 = (x2 + y2) % 2.
3. If p1 != p2:
   - For every cell (i, j): if (i + j) % 2 == p1 output 1 else output 2.
4. Else (same parity):
   - Choose a third color C = 3 (different from 1 and 2).
   - For every cell (i, j):
        * If (i, j) == (x1, y1) output 1.
        * Else if (i, j) == (x2, y2) output 2.
        * Else if (i + j) % 2 == p1 output 1 (any of the two colors, we pick 1).
        * Else output C.
The construction respects all adjacency constraints and uses the minimum possible number of distinct colors.

Complexities:
- Time: O(N·M) per test case (linear in the size of the matrix).
- Memory: O(1) extra besides the output buffer.
